coredata.dc:title: Preparation of separable MnFe2O4/ZnO/CQDs as a visible light photocatalyst for Gentamicin treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Rahal, Michel
Atassi, Yomen
Alghoraibi, Ibrahim
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, we report a method for the preparation of magnetically separable, visible-light activated ZnO photocatalyst for wastewater treatment from pharmaceutical drugs. The photocatalyst consists of Manganese Ferrite core, coated with Zinc Oxide, doped with N,S-Carbon Quantum Dots. The photocatalyst was characterized by XRD, HR-TEM, FT-IR, VSM, BET adsorption, UV–Vis absorption, and Photoluminescence. FT-IR confirmed the presence of distinct bonds characteristics of the different components. VSM showed decrease in magnetization of MnFe2O4 in the composite but can still be attracted to a magnet. BET results showed the surface area of photocatalyst is 22.56 m2 g−1, and pore diameter is 3.1 nm. Interestingly, the absorption of Zinc Oxide was shifted from UV range toward visible range. A decrease of PL spectrum was observed due to the suppression of defects emission. Electrochemical tests revealed reduced charge transfer resistance of the composite compared to pristine ZnO. The photocatalytic activity was evaluated by degrading Gentamicin antibiotic. Experimental data showed that Gentamicin degradation reached 69.1% during 2 h of radiation for 250 mg/L of catalyst concentration, and degradation rate constant was 0.0082 min−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Separable photocatalyst
Carbon quantum dots
Zinc oxide
Ferrite
Water treatment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g. technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support), but who do not meet the criteria for authorship, are named in the Acknowledgements and have given us their written permission to be named. If we have not included an Acknowledgements, then that indicates that we have not received substantial contributions from non-authors. The authors would like to thank the Higher Institute for Applied Sciences and Techmology for providing their facilties to conduct the current research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern for ZnO, ZnOCQDs and the composite.
HR-TEM images of (a–c) composite at different magnifications, d)-SAED pattern of the composite.
Elemental mapping in the composite, and EDX spectrum.
FT-IR spectra of MnFe2O4, ZnO and the composite.
Magnetization as a function of applied magnetic field for MnFe2O4 and the composite.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution (inset) of ZnO and composite.
UV–Vis Absorption spectra of MnFe2O4, ZnO and the composite.
PL spectra of ZnO and the composite.
EIS Nyquist plot of pristine ZnO and the composite, and equivalent circuit.
Cyclic Voltammograms of pristine ZnO and composite.
a)-Effect of composite concentration on catalytic degradation of GEN, GEN conc.: 50 ppm, b)- first order kinetics plot vs. irradiation time for fixed GEN concentration.
a)-Effect of initial concentration on catalytic degradation of GEN, composite conc.: 250 mg/L, b)-First order kinetics vs. time for fixed composite concentration.
Diagram of energy states with oxidation and reduction potentials.

head.ce:title: Preparation of separable MnFe2O4/ZnO/CQDs as a visible light photocatalyst for Gentamicin treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology, Damascus, Syria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology, Damascus, Syria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Damascus University, Damascus, Syria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Damascus University, Damascus, Syria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Arab International University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Basic and Supporting Sciences, Syria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Basic and Supporting Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Arab International University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Basic and Supporting Sciences, Syria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, we report a method for the preparation of magnetically separable, visible-light activated ZnO photocatalyst for wastewater treatment from pharmaceutical drugs. The photocatalyst consists of Manganese Ferrite core, coated with Zinc Oxide, doped with N,S-Carbon Quantum Dots. The photocatalyst was characterized by XRD, HR-TEM, FT-IR, VSM, BET adsorption, UV–Vis absorption, and Photoluminescence. FT-IR confirmed the presence of distinct bonds characteristics of the different components. VSM showed decrease in magnetization of MnFe2O4 in the composite but can still be attracted to a magnet. BET results showed the surface area of photocatalyst is 22.56 m2 g−1, and pore diameter is 3.1 nm. Interestingly, the absorption of Zinc Oxide was shifted from UV range toward visible range. A decrease of PL spectrum was observed due to the suppression of defects emission. Electrochemical tests revealed reduced charge transfer resistance of the composite compared to pristine ZnO. The photocatalytic activity was evaluated by degrading Gentamicin antibiotic. Experimental data showed that Gentamicin degradation reached 69.1% during 2 h of radiation for 250 mg/L of catalyst concentration, and degradation rate constant was 0.0082 min−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Advances in pharmaceutical science and drug development have improved the quality of life and patient care. It has recently been proved that good pharmacy practice has saved many lives during COVID-19 crisis. However, due to the massive increase in drug use, environmental problems have also increased. The major problem is the effluent of industrial or hospital wastewater [1], followed by the accumulation in environmental matrices [2]. For example, 92,700 tons of 36 different antibiotics were used in 2013 in China, estimated 54,000 tons were excreted to the environment [3]. On the long term, the accumulation have severe effects: It was observed gene expression alteration, abnormal protein and enzyme activities, and growth malformation in rats, fish and frogs [4]. In addition, the continuous exposure to antibiotics will make bacteria more resistant to treatment due to gene hypermutation [5]. Photocatalysis is one of the techniques used for wastewater treatment, which involves the use of semiconductors nanoparticles that generate free radicals when irradiated with external light source, these radicals can attack organic pollutants and degrade them [4]. Many semiconductors nanoparticles were employed as Titanium Oxide (TiO2), Zinc Oxide (ZnO), and Magnesium Oxide (MgO) [6–8]. Despite their characteristics, activity of pristine semiconductors is mainly active in UV range, using additional reagents as hydrogen peroxide, and long reaction time [9]. ZnO has been used in optical, electrical and pharmaceutical applications, due to its physical properties, low toxicity, chemical stability, and cost effectiveness. In addition, under different synthesis conditions, many nano morphologies can be obtained as nanotubes, nanorods, nanoflowers and nanospheres, [10–12]. ZnO energy bandgap is high: around 3.37 eV [13]. In addition, recombination of photo-generated electron/hole recombination is very fast, where surface reactions that generate radicals cannot optimally occur [14]. Recently, more researches have been developing visible-light or sunlight active photocatalysts, since solar light is renewable, emission-free source, and experiments can be performed under ambient conditions. The contribution of ultraviolet light to solar spectrum is only 4% [15], where activation of pristine ZnO will be weak. Many attempts reported to modify ZnO absorbance to visible light, as gold and silver nanoparticles doping [13], transition metal oxide doping [16], or using carbonaceous materials as graphene [17], but among all methods, there was no consensus on how absorbance is shifted toward lower wavelengths.

In practice, it has been found that the dispersion of the photocatalyst in the medium is more efficient than with immobilization on surfaces, since the contact area between pollutants and photocatalyst is greater. For example, direct contact with dispersed ZnO led to 98% removal of Methyl Orange within 40 min of irradiation, while only 74% of removal was achieved after 6 h when ZnO is immobilized on a membrane [9]. While separating photocatalyst from the medium by centrifugation is not convenient, the magnetic separation is more effective and can overcome the obstacle of remaining photocatalyst traces in the medium.

Herein, we propose a separable photocatalyst, composed of Manganese Ferrite, coated with Zinc Oxide (ZnO) and N,S-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), where CQDs will play a role of dopant for ZnO, and shifts the absorbance to the visible light. Gentamicin Sulfate (GEN) antibiotic was used as drug model. Since GEN can't absorb light in both UV and visible ranges, Ninhydrin method was employed to prepare a Gentamicin-Ninhydrin complex which has purple colour and can absorb light at 400 nm, which will facilitate drug degradation monitoring. For comparison, results were evaluated against pristine ZnO.

Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Merck, Diethyl Amine was purchased from Carl Roth, CTAB, Thiourea, Citric Acid and Ninhydrin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Gentamicin Sulfate was supplied from Dimas for Pharmaceutical Industries, HCl and NaOH were purchased from Panreac.

XRD patterns were registered by Philips X'pert diffractometer using CuKα (λ = 0.154 nm). UV–vis spectra were performed using Shimadzu UV-1800 Spectrophotometer. PL Spectra were performed using JASCO FP-8300 Spectrofluorometer. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer. Zeta Potential was measured using Horbia SZ-100 instrument. Magnetization was recorded at room temperature using Weistron VSM1100. N2 Adsorption-Desorption isotherms were recorded using BEL BELSORP Mini II at 77 K. HR-TEM was recorded using FEI Tecnai F20. Electrochemical tests were performed using IviumStat.h instrument. Elemental mapping was recorded using Tescan MIRA3.

Preparation of MnFe2O4 according to Ref. [18] with minor modifications. 10 mmol of MnCl2·4H2O was dissolved in a solution of 1 mL of 37% HCl solution in 4 mL of water, and 20 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in 40 mL of water. The two solutions were heated at 50 °C, mixed well for 30 min, then added to beaker containing 150 mL of DI water, and heated to 100 °C. Then 200 mL of preheated basic solution of 5% of Diethylamine (DEA), was quickly added to the solution under vigorous mechanical stirring. A black precipitate was formed, and constant stirring was continued for 2 h. Subsequently, the solution was cooled down to room temperature, and black precipitate was separated by a magnet, washed with DI water, and dried in closed oven at 70 °C for 24 h.
CQDs preparation was reported elsewhere [19].

1.0 g of MnFe2O4 and 100 mg of CQDs were dispersed in 150 mL of DI water, under mechanical stirring. The solution was heated to 75 °C. 0.9 mM of CTAB was added later to the solution as dispersant. 3 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was added. 50 mL of 5% solution of DEA was added dropwise, until pH reached 11. During ZnO formation, a 2 mL of 0.05 g/mL CQDs solution was added to the mixture. The stirring continued for 2 h at constant temperature. Thereafter, the solution was cooled to room temperature, and the precipitate was separated by a magnet, to remove unbound ZnO. The precipitate washed with DI water, dried in oven at 105 °C for 24 h. Pristine ZnO was prepared in the same procedure but without the presence of CQDs or MnFe2O4
MnFe2O4/ZnO/CQDs will be noted as composite.

The photocatalytic activity was examined by measuring the degradation of gentamicin, in pH = 7.0 at room temperature under LED visible light source (20 W). Different amounts of the composite were dispersed in 250 mL of Gentamicin solution and incubated in the dark for 60 min under mechanical stirring, to reach adsorption equilibrium. Then, light source was applied to the solution. Samples were taken at certain time intervals, where 5 mL of solutions was collected and treated with Ninhydrin solution as demonstrated in supplementary information, then degradation process was measured by absorbance decrease using UV–vis spectrophotometer at 400 nm. The pH of medium was chosen to be 7.0 since the composite has point of zero charge (pHpzc) at 6.2, and second pKa of Gentamicin is 7.5 (See supplementary information).

XRD experiments were performed to identify the crystal structure and estimate the particle size. XRD patterns in Fig. 1 shows identical hexagonal wurtzite structure, where for ZnO diffraction peaks indexed to 31.9° (100), 34.5° (002), 36.3° (101), 47.5° (102), 56.6° (110), 63.1° (103), 66.4° (200), 68.1° (112), and 69.1° (201) planes [15]. It can be seen that the addition of CQDs cause peaks broadening, and the intensity was decreased, same results observed for the composite.

For XRD calculations the instrumental broadening was excluded (see sup. Information). XRD peaks broadening is caused by internal micro-strain and crystallite size effects. From Table 1 , it is shown that ZnO crystallite size decreased, while the internal micro-strain increased. D-spacing, cell volume, a and b parameters were slightly increased, and values are identical to references [16,20]. This may indicate that structural changes emerged on both atomic and micro-scale, where ZnO growth in the presence of CQDs and MnFe2O4 resulted in low crystalline domains size and increased defects. Since peaks position didn't vary, it can be assumed that defects are non-uniform displacements of the atoms from their reference-lattice positions [21].

Fig. 2 shows TEM images of the composite particles, where aggregated grains have aspherical shape with sizes between 27.19 and 75 nm. According to mass contrast rule, since MnFe2O4 has the higher molar mass among the composite constituents, ferrite particles are shown as the darker particles, while ZnO are the lighter particles. MnFe2O4 particles were located within the ZnO particles, while CQDs spread over the surface of ZnO. Lattice fringes are shown in Fig. 2-c, where d-spacing of ZnO is 0.25 nm, consistent with XRD results. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) in Fig. 2-d illustrates diffraction rings are indexed to hexagonal ZnO crystal planes, in line with XRD data.

Fig. 3 shows elemental mapping, where elements are uniformly distributed in the composite, where composite particles are contoured in yellow. it can be seen that the elements C, Zn, O, S, Fe and Mn elements were homogenously distributed throughout the composite. In addition, results confirm that ZnO surface is well coated by CQDs.

Fig. 4 presents FT-IR spectra of MnFe2O4, ZnO, and the composite. For MnFe2O4, the band at 3394 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of –OH, and band around 580 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of the Mn–O–Fe linkage [22,23]. For ZnO, band at 3436 cm−1 corresponds to –OH stretching vibration, whereas the band at 1371 cm−1 is for OH bending vibration, and at 432 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of Zn–O bond [24]. For the composite, band at 3394 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of –OH, 1649 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of CO, at 882 cm−1 and 1139 cm−1 correspond to stretching C–O and in-plane bending vibration of C–H [25–27], and at 419 cm−1 corresponds to vibration of Zn–O bond. Carbon bond vibrations in the spectrum confirm the presence CQDs, while the Mn–O–Fe vibration didn't appear due to ZnO abundance. It is clear that the vibration of Zn–O bond was shifted to lower frequencies, which indicates formation of physical interactions between CQDs and surface layer of ZnO.

Fig. 5
demonstrates that MnFe2O4 shows a typical S-shape curve, which indicates a superparamagnetic property, with low coercive force (Hc = 0.02 kOe) and remanence (Mr = 3.6 emu.g−1), and the magnetic saturation (Ms) reached 36.4 emu.g−1. For the composite it is clear that Hc increased to 367 kOe and Mr is 5.5 emu.g−1, while Ms decreased to 17.33 emu.g−1, In addition, a hysteresis loop shape is appeared, a consequence for insertion of ZnO and CQDs with the ferrite, which they behave as diamagnetic materials; a higher coercive force is needed to re-orient residues of magnetic moments in MnFe2O4 with magnetic field to the zero. Practically, the composite still be separated from solution by using usual magnet (inset).

The BET and BJH analysis were investigated to obtain surface area, total pore size and pore diameter by using nitrogen adsorption isotherms. Results in Fig. 6 are in accordance with type IV isotherm with hysteresis loop of type H3. This isotherm type demonstrates the capillary condensation at higher relative pressures for mesoporous materials, where pores have a platelet-like shape, with size distribution between 2 and 50 nm [28].

Surface area of pristine ZnO is 27.41 m2.g-1, total pore size (inset) is 0.173 cm3.g-1, and pore diameter is 8.1 nm, while surface area of the composite is 22.56 m2.g-1, pore size is 0.105 cm3.g-1, and pore diameter is 3.1 nm. The reduced surface properties of the composite is due to the coverage of CQDs on the surface of ZnO.

Fig. 7 shows UV–vis Spectra. It is shown that pristine ZnO has sharp peak at 370 nm, attributed to intrinsic band gap transition between O2p valence band to Zn3d conduction band [29], corresponds to typical ZnO bandgap energy (3.3 eV). It is clear that the addition of CQDs has modified the absorption of ZnO, which led to development of a wide peak in the visible region, with maximum absorption at 437 nm, and a tail extends over the visible range. This can be ascribed to the doping of ZnO with sulfur and nitrogen which are components in CQDs that can shift the absorbance to the visible range [30,31], and carbon-based materials can generate long tail extends from the visible range to the near infra-red region [32]. It should be noted that the absorption of MnFe2O4 has no recognizable absorption in the visible range, which may not contribute to the absorption change to the entire composite.

Photoluminescence (PL) was employed since PL emissions are related to the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Fig. 8 shows PL spectra. For pristine ZnO, it has several emission peaks: intrinsic emission peak at 380 nm attributed to near band edge emission, caused by free excitons recombination, and three peaks appear in the visible region: at 425 nm, wide intense peak centred at 575 nm, and at 675 nm. Visible emission peaks attributed to surface and deep-level defects [33], which ascribed to various defects in the crystal structure as oxygen vacancies and zinc interstitials [34]. The peaks resulted from recombination of photo-generated hole and ionized charged state in the defects [35], and high peaks intensity compared to intrinsic emission indicates a rapid electron-hole pairs recombination. Interestingly, the composite showed the same PL emission peaks, but the intensity of visible region was decreased, indicating a slower electron-hole pairs recombination. This phenomenon occurs since the excited electrons of ZnO are trapped in CQDs, where π-orbitals of CQDs provide an additional path for charge carriers, which leads to enhance charge separation by preventing direct/fast recombination of electron/hole pairs [36–38]. PL spectrum of the ferrite shows no characteristic peaks, indicates that the ferrite doesn't contribute to PL properties.

Electrochemical tests were exploited to study charge transfer behaviour between composite-solution interface layer, as Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and Cyclic Voltammetry (CV). EIS was performed at AC frequency between 105 - 0.01 Hz. Fig. 9 shows Nyquist plot, fitted to simple Randles circuit, by using following elements: solution ohmic resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and double layer capacitance (Cdl). The radius of semi-circle represents charge transfer resistance (Rct), where at high frequencies, the intercept with real impedance axis represents the series solution resistance. It is shown that the composite responded with smaller semi-circle compared to pristine ZnO, where Rct values are 28.22 kΩ and 4.2 kΩ for ZnO and the composite, respectively. The smaller semi-circle radius indicates decrease in the interface layer resistance, which leads to higher efficiency of charge transfer, decrease in intragranular resistance, inhibits fast recombination between charges carriers, and enhancing photocatalytic activity [39,40].

The cyclic voltammograms plotted within the potential window of −0.6 V–0.4 V obtained at a sweep rate of 60 mV/s and pH = 7.4. Cyclic Voltammograms show typical quasi-reversible shapes, but the composite shows higher current density and enclosed area, compared to pristine ZnO, Fig. 10 . The enhancement of the current density reflects higher charge transfer ability, leading to enhanced electrical conductivity [41], which is mainly caused by the presence of CQDs.

Photocatalytic experiments were conducted by varying GEN and composite concentrations to investigate photocatalytic activity of the composite at different conditions. Concentrations of composite ranged between 100 and 750 mg L. Fig. 11 -a shows the degradation efficiency when composite concentration is 250 mg/L reached 69.1% after 2 h of radiation, the degradation efficiency decreased to 47.3% when composite concentration was 750 mg/L, it can be assumed that high composite concentration can prevent the radiation from penetrating the solution and reaching the internal composite particles. it is shown that when pristine ZnO employed, the degradation efficiency is 31.5%, which is the lowest among all experiments. The rate constants were calculated from Fig. 11-b for composite concentration between 100 and 750 mg/L, respectively: 0.00411, min−1, 0.0082 min−1, 0.0038 min−1, 0.0037 min−1, and 0.0011 min−1 for pristine ZnO. Due to higher efficiency of composite at 250 mg/L, it was used in following experiments.

Initial concentrations of GEN were 20, 50, and 100 ppm. Fig. 12 -a shows degradation efficiency for 20 ppm was 72.5%, which is slightly higher than 50 ppm, while degradation efficiency of 100 ppm was decreased to 51%. Rate constants for 20 and 100 ppm were 0.0095 min−1 and 0.004 min−1, respectively (see Fig. 12-b).

It is obvious that ZnO has higher adsorption capacity in comparison with the composite, this is ascribed to the reduced surface area of the composite, according to BET plots. In contrary, photocatalytic activity of the composite is much higher than ZnO, since the deposition of CQDs on ZnO surfaces has shifted the absorbance to higher wavelength; electron-hole pairs are photo-generated due to visible light source, while fewer electron-hole pairs are generated in ZnO since its absorbance band is mainly located in UV range. Shifting to higher wavelength is due to existence of N and S atoms in CQDs [42], where according to computational modelling, these atoms create electronic levels in the bandgap and responsible for absorption shift to visible range. As an example, N doping introduce N2p impurities states above Zn3d valence band of ZnO; hence, increases the visible light absorption. In such case, photogenerated electron-hole pairs exist between N2p and Zn3d bands, and similar effect was observed with sulfur doping, which can also generate modifications in light absorption [43,44]. In addition, the deposition of carbon materials on the surface of ZnO can promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, by prohibiting fast electron-hole pairs recombination, where CQDs serves as an electron reservoir to capture photogenerated electrons [45], thereby retarding the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus enhances the probability of creating reactive oxygen species (ROS), as hydroxyl and oxygen radicals, achieving better photocatalytic activity, compared to pristine ZnO. These results was confirmed by the PL intensity decrease [46,47].

In order to elucidate photocatalytic mechanism of the composite, valence band and conduction bands were calculated by employing Mulliken electronegativity theory [48]:
E
V B
= χ −
E e
+ 0.5
E g
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g
where
χ
is the electronegativity of ZnO (5.7 eV),
E e
is the energy of free electron on hydrogen scale (∼4.5 eV),
E g
is the bandgap energy and calculated by using [49]:
E g
= 1240 / λ
where
λ
(nm) is the maximum absorption in UV–Vis spectrum.

For ZnO It can be found that
E g
is 3.37 eV,
E
V B
is 2.885 eV, and
E
C B
is −0.485 eV, while for the composite
E g
is 2.85 eV,
E
V B
is 2.625 eV and
E
C B
is −0.225 eV. It is clear that bandgap energy was reduced, and both valence and conduction bands were shifted, but conduction band energy is still lower than H2O/ . OH formation potential, and the valence band energy is higher than O2/O2 .- formation potential, Fig. 13 . When the composite is excited by source light, excited electrons from conduction band will interact with dissolved oxygen from the medium, creating O2 .- radicals, while holes in valence band will oxidize H2O to create .OH radicals. Thus, generated radicals will attack adsorbed GEN on composite surface, initializing degradation reaction. A generalized reaction mechanism can be proposed:
Catalyst + hv → Catalyst(h+ (VB)+e− (CB))
h+ (VB) + H2O → *OH + H+
O2 + e− (CB) → O2 −*
GEN + h+ (VB) → GEN*
GEN + *OH → GEN*
GEN* → Low Mw products → CO2 + H2O
(h+ (VB) is the valence band hole, e− (CB) is conduction band electron).

Separable photocatalyst was prepared in order to remove Gentamicin from waste water. Using CQDs has several advantages, as narrowing band gap and preventing fast charge carrier recombination, although lowering ZnO surface properties and crystalline domains as shown in BET and XRD plots. Electrochemical tests revealed reduced charge transfer resistance and enhanced conductivity of the composite. The photocatalyst showed ability to degrade Gentamicin at different composite and GEN concentrations, where degradation efficiency was 69.1% for 250 mg/L of composite and 50 ppm GEN concentrations.

Michel Rahal: Conception and design of study, acquisition of data, analysis interpretation of data, Writing – original draft, Drafting the manuscript, revising the manuscript, critically for important intellectual content. Yomen Atassi: Writing and reviewing the manuscript, revising the manuscript, critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published. Ibrahim Alghoraibi: Writing – original draft, Drafting the manuscript, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g., technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support), but who do not meet the criteria for authorship, are named in the Acknowledgements and have given us their written permission to be named. If we have not included an Acknowledgements, then that indicates that we have not received substantial contributions from non-authors. The authors would like to thank the Higher Institute for Applied Sciences and Techmology for providing their facilties to conduct the current research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2022.126123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Survey on removing gentamicin antibiotic from aqueous media by recyclable magnetic titania photocatalyst
